original_abstract: This paper reviews the significant progress made over the last decade in the analysis of mobile phone datasets. This research field emerged from the increasing availability of massive, anonymized call records and has since evolved into a robust, multidisciplinary domain of its own. By treating mobile phones as ubiquitous sensors of human activity, researchers have moved beyond traditional survey-based sociology to observe communication and movement patterns at a societal scale. We survey the primary contributions to this field, focusing on the structural properties of social networks derived from call data, the modeling of individual and collective mobility, and the use of spatial information for geographical partitioning and urban planning. Additionally, we examine how these insights support international development and public health initiatives while addressing the critical challenges regarding data security and individual privacy.
humanized_abstract: Most networking science research has dealt with small-scale systems. Recently, mobile phone data has started to appear as new sources of interesting graph structures. The study of such data has become a major research area over the last decade. Analyzing mobile phone data started as an analysis of mobile phone call records. Large sets of anonymous call records become available and the social, spatial and temporal structure hidden in the data could be exposed and analyzed in details. This led to a major breakthrough in sociology since mobile phones can be seen as a sensor monitoring activities of individuals. For the first time in history sociologists are able to have measurements at the society wide level, rather than only basing their results from surveys on small groups of individuals. This survey attempts to summarize the most important results that have been achieved in the analysis of mobile phone data. First of all, we look at the social network structures that can be derived from call and messaging data; then we look at the behavior and patterns of individuals and groups when it comes to tracking mobility; finally we look at the aspects related to spatial information such as clustering and urban planning applications and their uses in aid of international development and public health while also discussing data protection issues.
